problem_summary: 2020 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy). The task: propose a global policy framework for Environmentally Displaced Persons (EDPs) — people whose homeland becomes uninhabitable from climate-driven sea-level rise — addressing both human-rights/survival and cultural-heritage preservation, then build models to (1) estimate the scale of the problem, (2) decide which countries should receive EDPs and in what share, (3) assess cultural impact, and (4) measure economic impact, using the analysis to justify the policy's necessity. This team's answer is the "Wandering Homeland Policy," supported by four chained quantitative models plus sensitivity analysis.
models_used: Model 1 - Cone-geometry EDP-count model: approximates the 4 SIDS (Maldives, Tuvalu, Kiribati, Marshall Islands) as a single right circular cone (base radius R0=20 km, base area 444 km^2, height h0=2 m). Linear sea-level rise dh = v*t with v=3.44 mm/yr submerges the cone from the base; new submerged annulus area dS(t)=pi[r^2(t-1)-r^2(t)] times a time-growing population density rho(t)=(1+gamma)^(t-1)*rho0 (gamma=2.3%) yields new EDPs/year. Scaled to world via the 65M-by-2070 SIDS figure -> 18,230,968 EDPs over 50 years.

Model 2 - Entropy Weight Method (EWM) multi-criteria allocation: objective weighting over 4 indicators (greenhouse-gas emission GG, GDP per capita, arable land per capita AL, renewable freshwater resources per capita RFR) across the top-50 GHG-emitting countries (40 yrs of World Bank data, computed in MATLAB). Derived weights [GG 0.259, AL 0.239, GDP 0.139, RFR 0.363]; scores rank countries, top-20 selected as receiving nations and their reception shares set proportional to weight.

Model 3 - Lotka-Volterra two-species competition model for culture: native culture (N1) vs EDP/foreign culture (N2) as competing species sharing one resource (population that identifies with the culture), normalized as population ratios with carrying capacities K1,K2 and interaction coefficients alpha12, alpha21. Two regimes: without protection (alpha21<alpha12 -> EDP culture goes extinct fast); with UN protection policy (alpha12 approx alpha21 -> EDP culture persists), demonstrating the policy's cultural value. Calibrated with Canada (logistic-fit 2070 population 3.8e7, 2.97e6 EDPs -> N1=92.7%, N2=7.3%).

Model 4 - Economic-impact / cost-benefit model: per receiving country i tracks per-capita resettlement cost A_i, cost of living C_i, and social value created M_i (assumed 0.75 x receiving-country per-capita GDP), with labor-capable fraction 65.33% (world 15-64 share) and a one-year delay before EDPs create value (modeled on EU one-year resettlement support). Integrates Model 1 EDP inflow over 50 years to produce per-country net income/expenditure curves and deficit-to-surplus crossover times.
Supporting: linear regression/least-squares fit of sea-level rise; logistic-curve population fit (Canada); single-parameter sensitivity analysis on v (3.04-3.84 mm/yr).
key_techniques: Model chaining / pipeline: Model 1's EDP counts feed Model 2's allocation, which feeds both Model 3 (per-country initial culture ratios) and Model 4 (per-country economic inflows) — a single coherent quantitative spine rather than four disconnected models.

Geometric idealization to make an intractable count tractable: collapsing four real island nations into one parameterized cone so submerged-population can be computed in closed form from sea-level rise.

Entropy Weight Method as an OBJECTIVE weighting scheme — explicitly chosen to avoid subjective/arbitrary weights, with a sanity-check narrative (high weights on RFR/GG/AL match intuition about survival-critical resources).

Cross-domain analogy transfer: repurposing the ecology Lotka-Volterra competition equations to model cultural assimilation vs preservation — turning a qualitative 'culture loss' worry into a quantitative ODE with a policy lever (the interaction coefficients).

Policy-as-lever modeling: each model encodes the proposed policy as a tunable parameter (protection raises alpha for EDP culture; allocation share = EWM weight; one-year EU-style support delay in economics), so 'with vs without policy' becomes a direct model comparison.

Honest segmentation of results: the economic model deliberately surfaces that ~half the receiving countries run a perpetual deficit, then feeds that finding into iterative policy improvements (5-year re-evaluation, a UNHCR-run EDP foundation, hardship exemptions).
MATLAB-based computation for EWM and the 20-country economic time series.
why_it_won: "From a judge's lens it does the three things F-problem winners must do. (1) It fully closes the loop the prompt demands: a named, articulable policy (the 'Wandering Homeland Policy', 8 numbered clauses) that is QUANTITATIVELY backed end-to-end — scale (Model 1), who-receives-whom (Model 2), culture (Model 3), and economics (Model 4) — so the policy is not hand-waving but is measured by the models, exactly what Problem F asks. (2) It shows modeling range and cleverness: an objective MCDM method (EWM) instead of arbitrary weights, plus a genuinely creative cross-domain borrow (Lotka-Volterra for culture) that gives judges a memorable hook. (3) It demonstrates modeling maturity by reporting an UNFAVORABLE result honestly (the policy hurts ~half the developing receiver countries) and then iterating the policy in response — judges reward teams that critique their own output and refine rather than oversell. The chained pipeline, named-policy framing, sensitivity check, and explicit improvements section together signal a complete, self-aware modeling cycle."
section_highlights: "Strong structural moves a future team can copy: (a) An 'Analysis of the Issue Paper' section up front with a mind-map figure that decomposes the policy problem into survival vs culture branches — frames everything that follows. (b) A named policy ('Wandering Homeland Policy') stated as 8 numbered clauses BEFORE the models, so every model visibly maps to a policy clause. (c) Explicit Assumptions blocks attached to each model (e.g., 'one culture per person', 'free trade / PPP equal', EDPs create value only after 1 year), making idealizations auditable. (d) A Notations table. (e) A dedicated 'Improvements and Weaknesses' section that does not just list limitations but turns each into a concrete policy upgrade (5-year periodic re-allocation, UNHCR EDP foundation, religion-aware placement, multilateral heritage cooperation). (f) Separate 'Importance and Strengths' section tying results back to international law and human rights. (g) Sensitivity analysis on the key driver v, with a clear 'trend unchanged -> stable' conclusion. (h) Result figures grouped by country archetype (best-case Canada/US/Australia; mid; perpetual-deficit developing) so the economic story is legible at a glance."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heroic geometric idealization: representing all SIDS as one 2-meter-tall cone with uniform density is physically crude — real islands are atolls/ring-shaped, not solid cones, and uniform interior population is unrealistic; the 18.23M figure rests on this and on a single linear sea-level rate.

Linear sea-level extrapolation: assumes constant 3.44 mm/yr for 50 years, ignoring well-documented acceleration of sea-level rise — likely a large under-estimate, which the team acknowledges only in passing.

Sensitivity analysis is thin: only ONE parameter (v) is varied, and over a narrow +/-0.4 mm/yr band; the much more impactful assumptions (cone shape, gamma growth rate, the 0.75-GDP social-value factor, 65.33% labor fraction, EWM indicator choice) are never stress-tested.

EWM weight interpretation is shaky: the method weights by data dispersion, not by causal relevance to 'who should receive refugees' — a high RFR weight (0.363) reflects variance across countries, not a defensible moral/logistic ranking, yet it drives the entire allocation.

Data-quality and reproducibility gaps: several figures appear schematic/placeholder (suspiciously linear EDP-per-year ramp, identical-looking economic curves, a stray '1921' year label), and the economic value parameter M_i = 0.75*GDP is asserted with no support.
Economic model omits discounting, second-order effects, integration costs beyond resettlement+living, and treats EDP labor value as if frictionlessly additive to GDP.

Lotka-Volterra culture model is illustrative rather than calibrated: alpha coefficients are set by assumption to produce the two desired regimes, so it argues the conclusion it assumes rather than estimating real assimilation dynamics.
reusable_patterns: Chain a 'how many' model -> a 'who/allocation' model -> 'impact' models, so a policy is justified by one connected quantitative pipeline rather than four standalone models.
For policy/F problems: state the policy as a small set of NUMBERED clauses early, then make each model the measurement of a specific clause; reviewers can trace policy-to-evidence directly.

Use an objective MCDM weighting method (Entropy Weight Method here; AHP/TOPSIS as alternatives) when you must assign country/option weights and want to pre-empt the 'your weights are arbitrary' critique — and add a one-line sanity check that the weights match intuition.

Borrow a well-understood model from another domain via explicit analogy (Lotka-Volterra competition -> cultural assimilation; SIR, logistic, predator-prey are similar candidates) to quantify a soft, qualitative requirement like 'cultural loss'.
Encode the policy itself as a tunable model parameter so 'with-policy vs without-policy' is a direct numeric comparison (the strongest possible argument for a policy's value).

Report an unfavorable result on purpose, then convert each weakness into a concrete, named policy improvement in a dedicated Improvements section — signals modeling maturity and self-critique to judges.

Geometric/closed-form idealization (collapse messy real geography into a single parameterized solid) to make a population/area count analytically computable — powerful but must be paired with honest caveats and sensitivity testing.
Segment results by archetype (best-case / mid / perpetual-loss) and present grouped figures so a complex multi-entity outcome reads as a clear narrative.

Structural scaffolding: Issue-analysis mind-map up front, Assumptions block per model, Notations table, Sensitivity section, plus paired Weaknesses/Strengths sections — a reusable template for any policy paper.
